chunks.text:
What menopause and perimenopause are
Menopause is when periods stop for good because hormone levels fall; perimenopause is the time before, when symptoms have started but periods have not yet fully stopped. They are a natural part of life and usually affect women between the ages of 45 and 55, though they can happen earlier.
chunks.title: Menopause and perimenopause

chunks.text:
Common symptoms
Periods often become irregular before stopping. Many women get hot flushes and night sweats — a sudden feeling of heat in the face, neck and chest, sometimes with sweating, palpitations or dizziness lasting a few minutes. Mental-health symptoms are common too: mood swings, low mood or anxiety, and problems with memory or concentration ("brain fog"), often made worse by poor sleep. Physical symptoms can include vaginal dryness and discomfort during sex, reduced sex drive, more frequent urinary infections and needing to pass urine more often, palpitations, headaches, joint and muscle aches, and changes to skin and hair.
chunks.title: Menopause and perimenopause

chunks.text:
When to see a doctor
Contact a doctor if you think you have perimenopause or menopause symptoms, especially if your bleeding pattern changes a lot, or if you have any vaginal bleeding after 12 months without a period. Treatments and self-care can reduce the impact on health, relationships and work.
chunks.title: Menopause and perimenopause

chunks.text:
What this guidance is
The WHO Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use (MEC), sixth edition, gives evidence-based recommendations on the safety of different contraceptive methods for people who have specific health conditions or characteristics. Its purpose is to help health workers support a woman to choose and safely use a family-planning method.
chunks.title: Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use
chunks.source: WHO — Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use, 6th edition (2025)

chunks.text:
How safety is decided
For each medical condition, a method is placed into one of four categories. Categories 1 and 2 mean the woman can generally use the method (the benefits usually outweigh any risk). Categories 3 and 4 mean the method should generally not be used, because the risk usually outweighs the benefit. Safety considers whether a method could worsen a condition or add health risks, and whether a condition makes the method less effective — always weighed against the benefit of preventing an unintended pregnancy.
chunks.title: Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use
chunks.source: WHO — Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use, 6th edition (2025)

chunks.text:
Methods covered
The guidance covers a wide range of methods, including low-dose combined oral contraceptives, the patch and vaginal ring, injectables (DMPA, NET-EN), progestogen-only pills, implants, emergency contraceptive pills, and copper and hormonal (levonorgestrel) intrauterine devices (IUDs). A woman should discuss her health history with a doctor or family-planning worker to find the method that is safe and right for her.
chunks.title: Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use
chunks.source: WHO — Medical eligibility criteria for contraceptive use, 6th edition (2025)

chunks.text:
What endometriosis is
Endometriosis is a chronic condition affecting roughly 10% of women and girls of reproductive age worldwide. Tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, causing inflammation and scarring.

Symptoms and impact
Many women have severe pain during menstruation and heavy bleeding. The condition often causes chronic pelvic pain that continues beyond the period, and pain during sex, bowel movements or urination. Up to half of women who struggle to conceive may have endometriosis. The pain can be debilitating enough to prevent work or school, and it is linked with fatigue, depression and anxiety.
chunks.title: Endometriosis

chunks.text:
Diagnosis, treatment and when to seek care
There is currently no cure, but symptoms can respond to pain-relief medicines (NSAIDs), hormonal contraceptives, and sometimes surgery. Diagnosis is often delayed by several years because symptoms vary and awareness is low. See a doctor for severe period pain, chronic pelvic pain, painful intercourse, or difficulty getting pregnant. Early diagnosis and care that combines medication, therapy and support can greatly improve quality of life.
chunks.title: Endometriosis

chunks.text:
Purpose
The WHO Consolidated HIV guidelines on service delivery bring together WHO's recommendations on how HIV services should be organized, so that care is more people-centred, integrated and fair, and so more people can get tested, prevented and treated.

People-centred, integrated services
The guidelines support delivering HIV testing, prevention and treatment in ways that fit people's lives — closer to the community, with less stigma, and joined up with other health services a woman may already use, such as antenatal (pregnancy) care, family planning and sexual-health services. Integrating HIV services with maternal and reproductive health helps reach women and prevent mother-to-child transmission.
chunks.title: HIV services (prevention, testing and treatment)
chunks.source: WHO — Consolidated HIV guidelines: service delivery (2026)

chunks.text:
Access and continuity of care
A central goal is to improve access, keep people in care over time (continuity of care), and reduce the steps and barriers between testing and treatment. HIV testing is confidential, and effective treatment allows a person living with HIV to stay healthy and prevents passing the virus to others. This is general information; a woman with concerns about HIV should speak with a health worker or visit a testing and treatment service, where support is confidential and non-judgmental.
chunks.title: HIV services (prevention, testing and treatment)
chunks.source: WHO — Consolidated HIV guidelines: service delivery (2026)

chunks.text:
Menstruation is normal
Menstruation is a natural monthly cycle, usually lasting 21 to 35 days. The lining of the uterus thickens, an egg is released around the middle of the cycle, and if pregnancy does not occur the lining is shed as menstrual blood. Most people menstruate without significant disruption to daily life; mild cramping, bloating and mood changes are common.

Common problems
More than two-thirds of people who menstruate experience menstrual pain, which can usually be eased with pain relief or heat. Nearly half experience heavy bleeding, which can lead to iron-deficiency anaemia. Symptoms that are unpredictable, severe or that disrupt daily life may point to conditions such as endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), fibroids or a bleeding disorder.
chunks.title: Menstrual health

chunks.text:
Hygiene and when to seek care
People who menstruate need clean water, private sanitation and clean absorbent materials (pads, cloth or reusable options), with safe disposal or washing. See a health worker for severe pain, very heavy bleeding, irregular cycles, or mood changes that disrupt work or school — these deserve a proper check.
chunks.title: Menstrual health

chunks.text:
What PCOS is
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common hormonal condition in which the body has higher-than-normal levels of androgens. It affects an estimated 10–13% of women of reproductive age, and up to 70% of those affected remain undiagnosed.

Common signs
Women with PCOS may have irregular or infrequent menstrual periods, difficulty getting pregnant, excess facial or body hair, hair loss, and acne. Symptoms vary a lot from person to person. PCOS is the most common cause of anovulation (no egg release) and a leading cause of infertility.
chunks.title: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)

chunks.text:
Diagnosis and management
PCOS is diagnosed by a health provider using menstrual history, blood tests for androgen levels, and sometimes an ultrasound of the ovaries — it cannot be confirmed from symptoms alone. Healthy lifestyle behaviours, including healthy eating and physical activity, are important for all women with PCOS. Medicines such as combined oral contraceptives can help regulate periods and reduce symptoms, and fertility treatment can help with conception. There is no cure, but symptoms can be managed. PCOS is also linked with a higher risk of insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes and heart disease, so regular check-ups help.
chunks.title: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)

chunks.text:
What postpregnancy family planning means
Postpregnancy family planning is the use of contraception after a pregnancy ends — either after childbirth (postpartum family planning) or after an abortion (post-abortion family planning) — by a woman who wants to avoid an unintended or closely spaced pregnancy. Spacing pregnancies gives the mother's body time to recover and is safer for both mother and baby.

Why it matters
The time around childbirth and after an abortion is an important opportunity to offer a woman information and a method of her choice, because she is often in contact with health services. Access to postpregnancy family planning helps countries make progress toward the health goal (SDG 3) and gender-equality goal (SDG 5), and supports a woman's right to decide whether and when to have children.
chunks.title: Postpregnancy family planning
chunks.source: WHO — Scaling up postpregnancy family planning: practical guide (2025)

chunks.text:
Quality and choice
Good postpregnancy family-planning services offer counselling, a full range of methods, and respect for the woman's own decision, with attention to quality of care and gender equality. A woman can talk with a health worker before or soon after delivery about which method suits her, including methods that are safe while breastfeeding. This is general information; a doctor or family-planning worker can help her choose what is right for her personally.
chunks.title: Postpregnancy family planning
chunks.source: WHO — Scaling up postpregnancy family planning: practical guide (2025)
